title: Add user auth, admin dashboard, and outgoing webhooks
summary: This is a deliberately dangerous PR and almost every cluster is a real defect, so I dropped nothing and instead collapsed the heavy duplication. The panel strongly converged on the critical security issues: committed production secrets (c0 + the two .gitignore-override findings), the `admin-master-key` JWT backdoor (c43/c24), the `?adminBypass=1` query backdoor (c2), email-suffix admin authorization spoofing (c47 absorbing all four adminController duplicates), the FakeDb SQL-injection + eval RCE (c26 absorbing the template-string and eval/YAGNI findings), the `new Function` filter RCE (c48/c9), MD5 password hashing (c25), full PAN/CVC being stored (c49/c3), logged (c50/c29), and CSV-exported (c58), webhook SSRF (c59/c34), and CORS `*`+credentials (c60/c35). JWT hygiene (fallback secret c42, missing algo-pin/expiry c44+c45, predictable reset token c46) all survive. I merged the four repeated admin checks into one DRY note (c13), the two module-singleton notes (c10), the two console.log-hygiene notes (c28), password-hash-in-response (c52/c37), login enumeration (c53/c31), password logging (c51/c27), unbounded admin reads (c21/c23), and the two weak test assertions (c38). I downgraded the blanket `@ts-nocheck` finding (c4) from must to should since it is a symptom of the code being deleted anyway, not an independent blocker. The performance set (c18, c19, c20, c22) and remaining quality items (c17, c32, c33, c36, c40, c46) are kept as distinct issues.

findings.title: Secrets checked into version control
findings.message: JWT_SECRET, ADMIN_API_KEY, and PAYMENT_GATEWAY_LIVE_KEY are now in git history. Secrets belong in a secret manager (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, GitHub Secrets) and injected at runtime via environment variables in CI/CD. Once a secret hits git, it is compromised forever — rotate all three immediately. Do not attempt to fix by removing the file; the history is permanent until the repo is nuked and recloned from scratch.

findings.title: Admin bypass accessible via query parameter
findings.message: Any request with `?adminBypass=1` grants full admin access. This is hardcoded, unconditional, and reachable in production. Remove this entirely or gate it behind a runtime environment check (`if (process.env.NODE_ENV !== 'production' && req.query.adminBypass === '1')`), but even then it's a dangerous pattern. This should not ship.

findings.title: @ts-nocheck disables safety on code with injection vulnerabilities
findings.message: The `@ts-nocheck` pragma blankets the entire file, which contains SQL injection vulnerabilities (template-string SQL, eval() in WHERE clauses). This hides type safety at exactly the point where it's most needed. Fix the underlying design instead of gagging the compiler.

findings.title: Module-level mutable singleton — hidden global state
findings.message: The `FakeDb` instance is module-level mutable state. All calls to `createUser`, `findUserByEmail`, etc. share this global store. This cannot be reset between tests, cannot be injected or swapped for a fake, and state leaks across test runs. Inject the store as a dependency or make it a parameter.

findings.title: Duplicated admin authorization check (listUsers)
findings.message: This method checks `email.endsWith('@admin.com')` inline. The same check appears in listTransactions (line 27–29), stats (line 42–44), and exportCsv (line 56–58). A `requireAdmin` middleware already exists in authMiddleware.ts (line 32–38). Apply the middleware to these routes and remove the inline checks.

findings.title: Direct function calls instead of dependency injection
findings.message: The controller directly calls `findUserByEmail`, `createUser`, `hashPassword`, `signToken` etc. as imported functions. These should be injected as dependencies (e.g., `constructor(private userService: UserService)`) so they can be tested, mocked, or swapped. The codebase already injects repositories — follow that pattern.

findings.title: Sync webhook firing blocks charge response latency
findings.message: fireForTransaction is awaited on the hot path after save but before returning to the client. If any webhook endpoint is slow or hung, request latency = charge_latency + Σ(webhook_latencies). With 100 webhooks at 200ms each, a charge becomes a 20-second operation. Webhook delivery should be enqueued asynchronously; return the charge response immediately, fire webhooks out-of-band.

findings.title: Unbounded webhook registration enables N×M cost
findings.message: There is no limit on how many webhooks a single user (or the system globally) can register. The fireForTransaction method loops through all webhooks sequentially with await for every charge. With 1M transactions and 1000 registered webhooks per user, this is O(N×M) webhook attempts. An attacker registers 10k webhooks, then each charge attempts 10k HTTP calls. Add a hard limit (e.g., 10 webhooks per user) and implement concurrent delivery with Promise.allSettled if you keep the sync pattern.

findings.title: Unbounded CSV export loads all transactions into memory
findings.message: exportTransactionsCsv calls findAll() with no limit, building the entire CSV in memory with [header, ...rows].join('\n'). With 1M transactions at ~200 bytes each, this is 200MB allocated per request. This OOMs the process. Implement streaming: yield rows in batches of 1000, write to res.write() as you go, never buffer the whole table.

findings.title: Admin transaction list returns unbounded data, no pagination
findings.message: listTransactions endpoint accepts an optional `filter` query param but has no limit, offset, cursor, or page size. It returns all matching transactions. With 100k transactions, even a filter returns potentially all rows. Every admin list endpoint (/admin/users, /admin/transactions) needs a bounded page size (default limit=50) with cursor- or offset-based pagination. As written, /admin/transactions?filter=status='failed' returns 100k rows.

findings.title: 5mb JSON body limit applied to all routes is a DoS amplifier
findings.message: The limit was bumped from 64kb to 5mb globally to support 'large CSV exports / admin payloads', but this applies to every endpoint. An attacker sends 5mb payloads to /charge, /auth/login, etc., forcing the server to parse large requests across all routes. Keep the global limit at 64kb; increase the body limit only for /admin/export.csv using app.post('/admin/export.csv', express.json({ limit: '5mb' }), ...) or a route-level middleware.

findings.title: MD5 used for password hashing
findings.message: MD5 is cryptographically broken. It's fast to crack and salted iteration counts don't help. Use bcrypt (bcryptjs for Node) or argon2 instead. This is a critical vulnerability if passwords are ever exposed.

findings.title: eval() on admin-provided WHERE clauses
findings.message: The `evalWhere` function replaces column names with stringified values and calls `eval()` on the result. An admin can break out of the WHERE clause context to execute arbitrary JavaScript. The comment 'Yes, we know' doesn't make it safe. Use a proper query parser or safe evaluator.

findings.title: console.log instead of logger for webhook delivery
findings.message: Multiple console.log calls (lines 30, 40, 42) should use the injected pino logger. This bypasses structured logging, log levels, and redaction. The project already uses pino throughout.

findings.title: Data leak: reset token presence reveals registration
findings.message: Password reset returns { ok: true } for non-existent emails but { ok: true, resetToken: '...' } for existing users. An attacker can enumerate registered emails by checking for the resetToken field. Always return { ok: true } without the token, and only send it via email.

findings.title: Weak input validation; no schema
findings.message: Manual truthiness checks instead of zod validation. No email format validation, no password length/strength rules. The project already uses zod (see paymentsController)—use it here. Missing: email regex, password min length, optional max length.

findings.title: Naming convention drift: snake_case vs camelCase
findings.message: UserRow uses snake_case (password_hash, created_at) but the rest of the domain (Transaction, domain objects) uses camelCase (cardLast4, customerId, createdAt). Pick one convention and use it consistently. Also causes bugs: csvexport refers to tx.cardLast4 but the header says card_last4.

findings.title: Weak assertion: toBeDefined()
findings.message: expect(res.body.token).toBeDefined() passes if token is any truthy value (including empty string or 0). Assert the actual contract: e.g., expect(res.body.token).toMatch(/^eyJ/) for a JWT, or expect(typeof res.body.token).toBe('string').

findings.title: Missing critical negative-path tests
findings.message: No tests for: invalid email format, duplicate registration, wrong password, non-existent user login, invalid/missing token on /auth/me, admin-only endpoints with non-admin user, password reset behavior. Auth is critical—test the failure modes.

findings.title: JWT_SECRET has insecure fallback
findings.message: If JWT_SECRET is not set in the environment, the code falls back to 'dev-secret-123'. In production, a missing env var means the pod signs JWTs with a known, hardcoded secret — any attacker can forge tokens. Use `required('JWT_SECRET')` or similar to crash the service if the secret is missing.

findings.title: Hardcoded JWT master key / backdoor
findings.message: If token === 'admin-master-key', any call to verifyToken returns an admin token. This is a permanent backdoor in the code. Remove it immediately. This is not a debug flag or a test utility — it is production code that anyone with access to the repository (or a deployed pod running this code) can exploit.

findings.title: JWT signed without algorithm pin or expiry
findings.message: jwt.sign() is called with no options — no `algorithm` or `expiresIn` specified. This means: (1) jwt.verify() has no algorithm pin and may accept 'none' or algorithm-confusion attacks, and (2) leaked tokens are valid forever. Use `jwt.sign(payload, SECRET, { algorithm: 'HS256', expiresIn: '15m' })` and verify with `{ algorithms: ['HS256'] }`.

findings.title: Password reset token uses predictable Math.random()
findings.message: generateResetToken concatenates Math.random() (not a CSPRNG) with Date.now() (attacker knows the second of the reset). Reset tokens become guessable. Use `crypto.randomBytes(32).toString('hex')` instead.

findings.title: Admin status granted by email suffix (spoofable)
findings.message: Any user whose email ends with '@admin.com' or '@honeybook.com' is treated as an admin. An attacker can register a user with email 'attacker@admin.com' and be granted admin access. Email is not an identity. Use an explicit allow-list, a group/role stored in a database, or an OIDC/SAML claim from a trusted IdP.

findings.title: User input evaluated with new Function()
findings.message: The filterExpr query parameter is passed to `new Function('tx', 'return (' + filterExpr + ')')`. An attacker can inject arbitrary JavaScript, e.g., `tx.amount; process.exit(1); //` to run code in the server. Do not evaluate user input as code.

findings.title: PAN and CVC persisted in transaction record
findings.message: The transaction object now stores `card_number` and `cvc`. CVC must never be persisted anywhere — PCI-DSS forbids it. PAN must be tokenized or encrypted, never stored in plaintext. Extract the last 4 digits (already done as cardLast4) and discard the rest immediately after authorization.

findings.title: PAN and CVC logged to stdout
findings.message: console.log('charge processed', JSON.stringify(transaction)) logs the entire transaction to stdout, including card_number and cvc. PCI-DSS forbids plaintext PAN in logs and forbids CVC anywhere. Replace with pino logger redacting card data: `logger.info({ transactionId }, 'charge_processed')` — the project ships a pino config with redaction allowlists for exactly this.

findings.title: Password logged in registration request
findings.message: console.log('register request:', JSON.stringify(req.body)) logs the entire request body including the plaintext password to stdout. Never log passwords or secrets. Remove this line.

findings.title: password_hash leaked in registration response
findings.message: The register endpoint response includes `password_hash` in the serialized user object. The password hash is the crown jewel — never serialize it. Omit it from the response (compare: login endpoint at line 53 correctly excludes it).

findings.title: User enumeration via distinct error codes
findings.message: Login returns 404 if user not found, and 401 if password is wrong. An attacker can iterate email addresses to discover which accounts exist. Return a uniform 401 (Unauthorized) for both cases: 'invalid email or password'.

findings.title: PAN exported in CSV
findings.message: The CSV export includes a `card_number` column (line 48). Card data must never be exported to CSV or any file format. If admins need transaction history, they should view masked data (last 4 digits only) in the UI.

findings.title: SSRF: unvalidated fetch to user-supplied webhook URL
findings.message: The webhook URL is user-supplied with no validation. An attacker can register a webhook with URL `http://169.254.169.254/...` (cloud metadata), `http://localhost:3000/admin`, or `file:///etc/passwd`. No protocol validation, no private IP range checks, and `redirect: 'follow'` allows redirects to internal endpoints. Validate the URL (scheme must be http/https only, resolve the hostname, block private/link-local/loopback ranges), and use `redirect: 'manual'`.

findings.title: CORS allows any origin with credentials
findings.message: `cors({ origin: '*', credentials: true })` advertises that the server will send authenticated responses to any cross-origin request. Browsers block this configuration, but misconfigured proxies or alternative clients may honor it, leaking credentials. Use an explicit origin allowlist: `cors({ origin: ['https://app.example.com'], credentials: true })`.
